content: Expected Shortfall (ES), also called Conditional VaR, measures the average loss in scenarios worse than the VaR threshold. While 95% VaR tells you the loss at the 95th percentile, 95% ES tells you the average loss in the worst 5% of cases. ES addresses VaR's key limitation: ignoring tail severity. Two portfolios can have identical VaR but vastly different tail risks—ES captures this difference. ES is a coherent risk measure (satisfies subadditivity), unlike VaR. Basel III replaced VaR with Expected Shortfall for market risk capital requirements in 2019, using 97.5% ES over 10-day horizon. Calculation requires more data than VaR and is more sensitive to extreme observations.
